title: The Epic
content:
At Francis Allenï¿½s on the Christmas-eve,ï¿½
The game of forfeits doneï¿½the girls all kissï¿½d
Beneath the sacred bush and past awayï¿½
The parson Holmes, the poet Everard Hall,
The host, and I sat round the wassail-bowl,
Then half-way ebbï¿½d: and there we held a talk,
How all the old honour had from Christmas gone,
Or gone, or dwindled down to some odd games
In some odd nooks like this; till I, tired out
With cutting eights that day upon the pond,
Where, three times slipping from the outer edge,
I bumpï¿½d the ice into three several stars,
Fell in a doze; and half-awake I heard
The parson taking wide and wider sweeps,
Now harping on the church-commissionners,
Now hawking at Geology and schism;
Until I woke, and found him settled down
Upon the general decay of faith
Right throï¿½ the world, ï¿½at home was little left,
And none abroad: there was no anchor none,
To hold by.ï¿½ Francis, laughing, clapt his hand
On Everardï¿½s shoulder, with ï¿½I hold by him.ï¿½
ï¿½And I,ï¿½ quoth Everard, ï¿½by the wassail-bowl.ï¿½
ï¿½Why yes,ï¿½ I said, ï¿½we knew your gift that way
At college: but another which you had,
I mean of verse (for so we held it then),
What came of that ?ï¿½ ï¿½You know,ï¿½ said Frank, ï¿½he burnt
His epic, his King Arthur, some twelve booksï¿½ï¿½
And then to me demanding why? ï¿½Oh, sir,
He thought that nothing new was said, or else
Something so said ï¿½twas nothingï¿½that a truth
Looks freshest in the fashion of the clay
God knows: he has a mint of reasons: ask.
It pleased me well enough.ï¿½ ï¿½Nay, nay,ï¿½ said Hall,
ï¿½Why take the style of those heroic times?
For nature brings not back the Mastodon,
Nor we those times; and why should any man
Remodel models? these twelve books of mine
Were faint Homeric echoes, nothing-worth.
Mere chaff and draft, much better burnt.ï¿½ ï¿½But I,ï¿½
Said Francis, ï¿½pickï¿½d the eleventh from this hearth
And have it: keep a thing, its use will come.
I hoard it as a sugar-plum for Holmes.ï¿½
He laughï¿½d, and I, thoï¿½ sleepy, like a horse
That hears the corn-bin open, prickï¿½d my ears;
For I rememberï¿½d Everardï¿½s college fame
When we were Freshmen: then at my request
He brought it ; and the poet little urged,
But with some prelude of disparagement,
Read, mouthing out his hollow oes and aes,
Deep-chested music, and to this result.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
